CURRENT AFFAIRS.

We sce that on New Year’s Day the Irish T'imes in
its leading anticle rubbed the Mid-Cork salt into “ Na-
tionalist »> Ireland. We don’t blame it in one sense,
‘for that Scotch paper in Ireland is naturally delighted
at the sight of dissention. It remarks:—The Mid-
Cork election has an obvious moral for the present
Government. It will confirm Irish Unionists in their
resolution to defend their best interests against the
attacks of men who could not be trusted to agree even
in misgoverning Ireland.” Arnott’s paper does its best
to make Unionist and anti-Irish capital out of the
squabble, but, anyway, the Ascendancy of the Unionist
faotion in Ireland-is on the slippery slope, and Amotit’s

- paper is bound in time to slip wuth it, unless it suc-
ceeds in doing a Vicar-of-Bray turn.

- For our pant, we look with critical but not un-
friendly eyes at the Irish Parliamentary Party. Ab
best ey are but a department amongst the varied and
* complex forces making, or trying to make, for Imsh
Nationhood.  If they' are efficient as a party Wwe

/

think they are at present worthy of the support of
the country. And there is no doubt that those, like our-
selves, who seethemin their national perspective, and
therefore, see them small in relation to other forces,
feel somewhat extra considerate towards them by reason
of a sort of revulsion against the exaggerated and ab-
surd attack made on them by verbose extremists’’
who perhaps not being used to dealing with ideas, are
inclined to ride to the devil on one when they happen
to lay hold of it.

At its best, the Parliamentary Party is looked on only
as a department by thinking Irishmen. There is no
room in it for splits. Even if Mr. O’Brien succeeded
in sawing off what he might call the “ rotten branch,”
he should not forget that he, too, is sitting on the
branch, and bo would come to the ground like poor
Tommy Kavanagh, the handyman of Castleknock—God
rest his soul—when he was sent to prune a tree in the
College grounds. The people are not in a mood to
trouble over much about the M.P.’s. Am efficient
Party has its work cut out for it, as representing a
department of National effort, to hold the attention
and retain the support of the political section of the
country: Mr. O’Brien on the one side, and the
bosses,”” whoever they may be, on the other, may suc-
ceed in belittling cue another, but in so far as they
emphasise the lack of efficiency on the part of Parlia-
mentarians as a class, they likewise succeed in weaken-
ing the ncue too tough National branch upon which
they are all perched.

As for the Unionists as represented by the “Zrish’’
Times, their crow is a rather shortsighted one—if we
may put it that way. Looking at the matter as far as
we can from the Unionists point of view—which is the
point of view of the skins and stomachs of that bundle
of petty persons collectively called Irish Unionists—we

are inclined to think that an efficient Iarliamentary _ -

Party would be, at least, the lesser of two evils for
them. Their day of ascendancy is done however the
pendulum swings. They will get justice from TIreland
in any case; but naturally what they are anxious for,
once their ascendancy is knocked, is generous and
liberal treatment. Take care they would not fare
better under Home Rule than they will fare if the
hopes of the Irish people are bluntly deferred once
movre. The economic position: of the anti-Irish element:
is weak except in the North-Fast patch, where Belfast
is. Once the land is taken from them, what economic
power have they? In the distributing line they have,
to some exbent, more efficiency than the Nationalists,
but they attained that relative position by the operation
of the forces of Ascendancy one way, and the forces of
the Penal Laws the other way. They cannot count
upon that lasting, for the Nationalists are thinking
now, and are tackling economic problems in the con-
crote. Nationalists of the coming gemeration, even of
the generation that is now arriving on the business
scene, are able to run first-class grocery and provisions
distributing establishments as well as those who, for
one reason or another, had rather a monopoly of that
and other olasses of distributing business.  The
Irish Times may chuckle at the dissentions amongsh
Nationalists, bub it cannot keep the wind in the pricked
balloon of ascendancy all the same. If we might give
advice to the Unionists, we would suggest to them
that the sooner they try to cultivate an Irish mind and
attempt to look out on Ireland through Irish glasses, -
the better for themselves materially—we won’t go fur-
ther than materially, for we are too sensible to waste
our breath whistling jigs to a milestone, and you must.

negotiate with the stomach and not »wibh f,'bf’ brain of

an Irish Unionist.

We wish Bung a leisurely new year! The new. re-
gulations are in force now, and the glory of “the
Cuckoo > as of Clontarf is somewbat tarnished. Pooc
Bung! The nation does mot weep for you!  Next
Saturday Mr. B. must put up his shutters at .ten
o’clock in Dublin, and at five on Sundays; and the
thirsty traveller from Cuffe Street will have to “travel”
five weary miles before he can legally claim to have a
bona thirst.  Booterstown, where is thy fame?—it is
gone in the wind! But what about next St. Patrick’s
Day? Is there no chance of thoe Parliamentary Party
getting that little Bill through in time? By the way,
as we are speaking on the drink question, may we ask
when we may expect the statement of accounts. of the
All-Treland Bazaar? That bazaar, it will be. remem-
bered, came off during the year before last, and it is
high time that the statement of accounts were issued.

A reader of ours in Cork sent an American cousin of
his a copy of Cork’s “only national,”’ the Ezaminer.
In the course of a letter, thanking our Cork reader, the
American says, It contains. more American- news than
any of the papers over here!’”

We have received No. 1, Vol. 1 of The Vindicator,
a new weekly halfpenny paper started in Belfast. Tt is
described as “a journal to vindicste the- Catholic and
National Cause.”” We only know Belfast as. outsiders,
and as outsiders we wonder that the Catholic: and
National forces do not pull together with an unanimity
that it might be unreasonable to expect even. in other
parts of Ireland. For the Catholics and Nationalists.of
Belfast are a minority, surrounded by a. hostile, anti-
Catholic and anti-Irish population.  How far Mr.
Bung may be at the bottom of local difficulties we can-
not say, but Mr. Bung is influential in Naticna
circles in Belfast, and probably Mr. Bung who vends
beer indiscrimately to the Orange and the Green alike,
has his own very vulnerable points in Belfast. The
so-called Nationalist daily paper, the Zrish News, leaves
much to be desired any way; and we hearty welcome a
new champion of Nationalist and Chatholic opinion.
Our new contemporary leads off with the following
paragraph : —

[ journal is founded for the settled purpose of vindcating
hts of the Catholic citizens. of Belfast. That is. our justi-
fication. We rest our claim to the support of our Catholic fellow-
citizens entirely on tha fact that the time has now come when
Catholic interests in Belfast can only be entrusted to a journal
whose principles are: Catholic, first and last—to a journal which i3
urfettored by the trammels of political expediency, or partizan
time-serving, and which has for its aim and object solely the
vindication of the claims of the. Catholies of this city to fair and
equitable treatment in all matters touching -their interests as
Catholics. That is a plain and definite purpose, and we are
confident that it commends itself to every right-thinking Catholic
in Belfast.

We: quote also the following further extvact from the
leading: article in. the first number of: our contemporary :

We: disavow, once and: for all; anvthing approaching bigotry, or
anything- calculated to give offence to the professors of any other
religious belief. That is the very last thing we would desire.
But the position of: Catholics in Belfast is one which justifies the
course which we have determined to take. We Catholics number
over 83,000 of the inhabitants of this great Northern city. Until
1896, when the Catholics of Belfast combined together and enforced
their right to representation on our public Boards, they were in
the position of mere unenfranchised serfs, witliout any voice in
the government of: the.city. to the: rates of: which they. were bound:
to. contribute.. They suffered all the evils which accompany:
Taxation without Representation. By an. electoral, system which
2 Patliamentary Committee in 1896 declared to be unfair, they
were excluded from any effective participation in the public affairs
of the city. How Catholics won even a small share in municipal
government is well known. The Belfast’ Catholic: Representation’
Commiittes, after a hard fight, and at much expense; obtained a
re-arrangement of tho. wards: of: the: city. in: such a way. that, at:
last, Catholics werc: granted a small share in: the. government of
the affains of the city. That.concession was: made to.the Catholics.
of Belfait solely and entirely on the basis that.it was unfair and
inequitable that.the Catholic minority in the city sliould bave no
share in the representation on the public Boards. The Catholic
minority won that oconcession solely” and' entirely as” Catilolics:
From: beginning to end of the negotiations. which led up to the:
eoncession the demand: was made for Catholic Represcntation.

THE LEADER.

January 5, 1907.

Our new Belfast contemporary is only a four-page
paper, but no doubt even though it sells for a §d., it
will grow in size if it gets the encouragement, which.
we think it-deserves. We hope that it will cater for
ghe Trish Irelanders of Belfast—a growing body we have
reason to believe. Zhe Vindicator, we take it, will be
chiefly concerned with local Be‘lfnast affairs, and without.
wishing to commit the indiscrebion of poking our noses
into other people’s business, of which business we are
only very imperfectly informed, we would commend
The Vindicator to the consideration of all our many
Catholic readers in Belfast.

A provincial trader writes to us— Will any readers
of THE LEADER kindly say if they know any Irish firm
who advertise their goods on match boxes I have
just been offered matches at a ridiculously low figure
with an advertisement of ’s Milk.  As matches
are an item- which are usually retailed at a loss (large
quantities are given away for nothing), perhaps some
Irish manufacturer wouRl step into the breach, and
advertise his wares in the manner suggested; the
‘quid’ on the part of the manufacturer would he the
advertisement his goods would get at the hands of the

- retailer, whose ‘quo’ would be represented by prices
less than half what he is at present paying.”

A local hockey club gave a fancy dress ball at Dun-
dalk, and a local paper informs its readers that:—“We
are not in a position to give a description of the cos-
tumes indulged in as we were not there. The golden
‘age, the heroic age, and we may say all the ages, were
represented.”’  The phrase “all the ages’ ir rather
hard, surely, on some people; might not our contem-
porary have shown more consideration for the feelings

of the gay old bachelors of the one-leg-in-the-grave
type, and that-of the wallflowers of “all the ages?’
By the way, the phrase “ the costume indulged in™’ in
connection with a Dundalk hockey ¢ fancy dress ball *
is not bad.

Under 'the heading  Broadford Notes’’ our contem-
porary, the Clare Champion, asks:— We have eleven
police stationed here presently instead of four as here-
tofore, and we would like to know what are they doing
while such outrages are being committed under their
very noses, or are the people of Broadford to suppose
we have another Sheridan in our midst?’  Perhaps
one of them got a present of a new concertina by way
of a Christmas box.

Irish names for Irish children is an aspect of the
Irish-Treland question that has often been touched
upon. From the offices of -tHe “Irish Messenger of
the Sacred Heart,”” 3 Great Denmark Street, Dublin,
we have received “ A Calendar of Irish Saints.”” In
the preface we read—" Oue of the reasons for drawing
up this list of our saints is to enable those who would
like Irish names or their English equivalents for their
children at Baptism or Confirmation, or else for their
residences, etc, to find suitable ones with less d-fiiculty
than has been hitherto the case.”” In some cases as
many as four or five saints’ names are given for a
single day. The price is only one penny, and the little
book ought to have a large circulation.

One would think that in view of the experiences of
the Post Office and the “ National >’ Bank that Thom'’s
Directory would be chary of standing out against Irish
Ireland. But Thom’s have stepped into the breach.
Here is part of the story:—“ About the middle of
November last—the time for sending in particulars to
the Directory for publication—I visited their office to
put the name, address, etc., of the local branch of the
Gaelic League in, with some other information includ-
ing my own name and addresses. The clerk took down
all information till we came to my own name. This
I always spell in the Irish or correct manner, having
put aside the English corruption some five years ago.
The Christian name I also spell in the Irish way. The
clerk did not understand the way I pronounced it, so it
had to be written for him. He looked at it, and
entered it down, when it evidently struck him that it
might be Irish, for he asked that information. ‘It is
my way of spelling the name,” I said, ‘it happens to
be also the Irish way.” He scratched out the name,
and was about to translate it, saying he could not put
in Trish names. I told him that that was my way for
spelling my name. It mattered nothing to him were
it the Irish or the Emglish way. He had mo right to
give me directions as to the spelling of my own name,
a matter that one’s self ought to be more familiar with
than -anyone else. This sort of reasoning was of no
cffect. The name was Irish, that was enough—could
not be put in on any account. He would write it in
English. Nonsense; you spell my name my own way or
not at all. Have you the insolence to give me direc-
tions as to the particular letters that form my own -
name? The idea is absolutely ridiculous. If a German

came in as I do, would you translate his name into

English? Nothing to do with it, he would not have
the Irish, but would put in a ‘translation’ if T liked.

—

Do you persist in your ridiculous refusal? He did.
‘Well, let me see your manager.”

* Qur correspondent saw the manager, and a somewhat
similar scene was gone through. The manager stated
that he could not make the Directory bi-lingual.  Well
though that question did not arise, we may say that we
see 1o reason why Thom’s Directory should not be
bi-lingual. In the case in question, the matter simply
was a refusal on the part of Thom’s autocrats
to accept for their Directory an Irishman’s name.
There was even no question of Irish type, for the name
vould be printed in ordinary Roman letters. Our cor-
respondent remarks :— There are, as far as I can see,
three ways of bringing this old-fashioned firm up to
date, the last of which, however, is not very feasible'—
(1) All the Irish Irelanders of the country to send in
their names, at least, spelt as they usually spell them,
and upon refusal to withhold them in any form; (2)
To approach the Government with a view to withhold-
ing the assistance the postmen give the firm, and mqke
inquiries e the Government’s printing contract which
Thom’s get every year. See if it is obtained with or
without competition; (3) To start a new Directory.”

Window-cleaning does not strike one as a highly
skilled craft; one would be inclined to think that even
technical education could have little or no bearin
upon it, and that even the acknowledged lack of tech- oy
nical education in this country would in no way mili-
tate against our producing our own window-wipers.
And who ocares whether his window-cleaner is an
Adonis or has a squint in his eye? Window-cleaners
are necessary nuisances about a house or an office, and :
for our part we have frequently postponed calling them ‘
in, preferring to put up with dirty windows for a few ‘
further days. But their lack of beauty never in- i 1
fluenced us. We take the following advertisement 3 |
from the ZLiverpool Daily Post of Monday last:— B
“ Window Cleaners, experienced, used to contract work, ‘
should apply now and again by letter to Dishers, Win-
dow Cleaners, 6 Fleet Street, Dublin. TUseless .men
writing without copy of latest reference as to honesty
and experience; also state age and wages required
(photo if possible).” So it would appear that Dublin,
to go no further in Ireland, is not equal to supplying
Dishers with window-wipers, even in these days of |
scarcity of employment, and even those replying from
Liverpool are requested to send their photographs!

WANTED—ANOTHER COMMISSION.

HE sworn inquiry called for by the Cork Lunatic
Asylum Board will, it is certain, result in some
interesting disclosures concerning the system of Con-
tracting for Public Institutions, and will, perhaps,
suggest to our wise Governmental authorities the need
for yet ancther Commission! The “Public Contracts
Commission,”” I venture to predict, whem, it comes to
take evidence in some Irish cities and country towns,
will provide lively and entertaining copy for our jour-
nals, and exceedingly inberesting reading for bhe
general public. And the sooner the new Commission
is pubt “sitting’’ the better for the gaiety of the
mation.

The ‘“Lccal Contracts’’ are, in Irish towns, “the
catch of the season,” and when they are announced
the excitement becomes very apparent in the drapery
and other shops, and patterns and specifications, etc.,
ete., are dispatched “for the very keenest quotations *’
to the wholesale warehouses and manufacturers. Every
Dark Brother is bombarded, and every possible argu-
ment put forward pleading for a “special cut ’—im-
deed the Commander of the Fleet himself could learn
a lesson from the intending contractors when it comes
to the Autumn Contract Manceuvres!

It is, however, when the Comtracts are given that the
fun begins, then there is weeping and gnashing of teetih,
and, on the other hand, justifiable jubilation, for Mr.
O. has lost by a y};th of a penny, and Mr. Mac has the
supreme pleasure of seeing his name in print as the
lucky contractor for 50 yards of calico at a price which
his oppoment declares “ is a dead loss.”’

To come to the point at once—The method of Con-
tracting for Imstitutions is, in this writer’s opinion,
decidedly out of date, and leads to all sorts of irregu-
larities. In the vast majority of workhouses, etc., the
“censor ’”’ for comtracts is the *master,” and it is
very obvious that he cannot be an expert in all things.
In some workhouses there is a “ Committee”” of a few
members, and in large institutions there is a Store-
keeper. A few examples from the writer’s actual
knowledge of how these people handle comtracts will
interest the public, and, I am sure, draw from LEADER
readers some even more interesting experiences.

One of the “ censors’’ in a certain institubion has an
oniginal method of testing the strength, or untear
ableness” of tweed. Gefting equal lengths of cloth
from the standard sample,”’ and from the ecloth

K CORK BREWERS’ CLUB.

A CASE in connection with the administration of
£\ the Clubs Act: was recently heard at the Cork
Police Court, which appears to have escaped: the notice
of the Dublin Press. Some few months ago the em-
ployees of Messrs. Beamish and Crawford, brewers,
started a- social club on premises almost adjoining the
brewery concerns, and applied-for the usual club license
or registration before the City Police Court.

During the hearing of the application it transpired
that this club was established with the ostensible object
of affording social recreation and amusement to the
members, and to all intents and purposes was:to be a-
haven of rest and enjoyment for them after their day’s
toil; all that was required to complete their happiness
was permission from the British' Government-to have'in-
toxicating liquors' supplied within the club premises:
Sir Edward Fitzgerald, who happened to be en the
beuch, and' who, notwithstanding what some critics say
about' him, has' a-Happy knack of calling a spade a
spade, at once submitted the innocent-looking: scheme
to some. vigorous searclings:.

Having ascertained that the president (as well as -

principal organiser) ofsthe proposed club was the manag-
“ ing director: of Messrs: Beamish’s Brewery Co.—viz.,

JANUARY 5, 1907.

Mr. R. H. Beamish, who also happens to be our City
High Sheriffi—he dilated on the perilous responsibilities
undertaken by the promoters of the club in attempting
$o afford facilities for drinking at late hours of night
o brewery employees, who, above all other men in the
world, had during the day ample opportunities of slak-
ing their honest thirst. Those men, he furthermore said,
would not merely take drink themselves at late hours,
but would afford temptations to their workingmen
friends to drink with them.

After some further discussion on the merits of the
<ase, it was decided to adjourn the application to a
future date. On the day of the adjourned hearing our
police, office presented an unusually animated appear-
ance, for not alone were some strange faces to be seen
in the body of the court, but the bench, which usually
comprises about two or three magistrates, was we'l
‘occupied, showing that the crack of somebody’s whip
was responded to (it is marvellous what bits of clay
some of our independent Justices become in the hands
«of certain people), and that during the period of
adjournment the wires were busy between Southgate,
South Mall and Patrick 8t. Mr. R. . Beamish was
also in Courb to testify to the untold blessings which
this projected symposium was to shower on his retainers
and: dependents, and although Sir Edward Fitzgerald
Teiterated his former objections, but in still more
forcible terms, the majority of the bench held that
Messrs. Beamish and Crawford’s self-sufficient brewer-
in-chief was a public benefactor, and was entitled to
have his club registered in compliance with the Act.
Public attention was at once centred in this high-
handed decision, and tho Father Mathew Temperance
Bociety decided to raise an objection to it when it came
up for confirmation.

Accordingly, on the third day of hearing Mr. J. J.
Horgan, solicitor, and some members of the Total
Abstinence Society appeared in Court, and, to the
«dismay of those enlightened and disinterested Justices
-of the Peace, pointed out that their previous decision
was. quite illegal on the grounds—(1) that the president
of the club, My. Beamish, was intercsted in the sale of
<drink; (2) that the draft rules made no provision for
the payment of members’ subscriptions ‘welve months
in advance, but, on the contrary, that the membership
fees were to be stopped every week out of the wages of
“the workingmen on what is equivalent to a truck system.
:So that according to the latter provision an employee
of this firm was supposed to become #pso fucto a member
-of. this jollification club ; at least, if he should be strong-
headed enough to resist, it could be made hot for him

. by the brewer-in-chief. That is certainly an extra-
~ordinary situation, and looks like a relic of what I might
call true-blue feudalism. There has been in existence
in Corlc for some years a brewery workmen’s society, to
5 which, I am informed, a large number of Messrs.
Beamish and Co.’s employees belong. This society has
“been doing meritorious work for its members, and in
-their rooms no intoxicating liquors are sold. As far as
‘I know, there is no desire on the part of tho members
to have it dissolved, and perhaps Mr, Beamish may be
“induced to reach them his benevolent hand instead of
“worrying himself about establishing an extra tap-house
at his brewery gate.

At all events, his drinking club is knocked on the
head, and the employees of the concern are to be con-
-gratulated on having been saved from themslves as well
-as from the pitfalls into which the vanity and ambition
of their master must have inevitably led them. Con-
gratulations also to Mr. Horgan and his clients for the
able and manly fight they have made against the occult
“influences set at work to provide owr community with
-an additional liquor-distributing: agency, and that of
“the worst- and most objectionable type.

THE LIBRARY QUESTION.

A. N ounce of practice is, any day, worth a pound
of theory, and in these anticles of mine I have
been endeavouring to keep out of the region of ti
mere imagination and to confine myself to solid fac
of which I had personal knowledge or trustworthy
formation, and to reasonable dedudtions drawn thers-
from. I have no desire to obtain a petty triumph over
any of my friends, for, however widely we may diffsr
in our views on this Library question, we have—t-
bulk of us, at all events—the same ultimate object in
view—the temporal and ebernal intemests of this Niobe
of nations of owrs and its down-trodden and long-per-
secuted people. Far be it from me to endeavour tv
impose my views on anybody, or to seek to argue from
only one or two particulars to the general. I have in
the main been merely giving my practical cxperi
on the question at issue to yowrself, Mr. Editor,
to your readers—the modb level-headed and intellige
clientele that, I fancy, any journal in Ireland can ¢
mand this momenft—with the view of enabling all
form an impanitial and dispassionate judicial opinion re-
garding it. An enlightened jury and an impartial
judge base, the verdict of the one and the decision of
the other, not on the special pleading or the flo
analtory of advocates, but on the evidence of witnessss.
There is, however, one thing that I and cthers in the
provinces strongly object to and resent, and it is that
any self-constituted body of gentlemen in the metrc-
polis should, however well meaning and learned they
may be, and by whatever corporate name they may
choose to be christened, settle themselves down to gra-
tuitously dictate to us how we should establish and
manage our rural libraries, and, through certain orga:s
in the Press, to shove their opinions—so to speak—-
down our very throats. It would be well that the
gentlemen should remember that though they may ex-
in University distinotions—those comet-like tails
that spread out behind so many of them, and in which,
granting there is good solid matter in ‘the nucleus,
there may be little but gas—yet they have not, neces-
sarily, a monopoly of natural ability or commonsense,
and, further, may—many of them—be utterly wanting
in worldly experience. Their ideals and their theories
may no more work out in practice—where it is the
unexpected that so often happens—than would, say,
our old friend, Mr. Oldham’s hallucinations in political
economy.
Curiously enough, just this time three yzars ago I
devoted a whole Uhristimas holiday to an endeavour to
extricate many of those same well-meaning gentlemen,
young and old, from a mire into which they had got in
connection with a most laudable but ill-organized move-
men, which, T believe, had its very origin in the insti-
tution where this Free Library project appears to have
got its most influential platform. I happened at the
inception of the movement in question to be in clase
touch with, and in fact acted in the capacity of con-
sultor to, one of the ringleaders. The programme, in
proof, was submitted for my comments thereon, and
these I gave unreservedly, pointing out, amongst other
things, that if certain principles were persisted in they
must, from my kmowledge of the wicked world we live
in, inevitably lead to disaster. But my advice w
unheeded. At the eleventth hour when I saw that the
blow was about to fall, though the promoters were stiil
blind to the impending danger, I took off my coat and
“worked like a black ”’ to avert it. But even then the
case I made was not pub anything like as quickly. be-
fore the public as was necessary, amd as I suggested,
and the crash I had from the first. foretold, came.
Now, I do hope that, forewammed by the past, our
friends will not in their inexperience force us all into
a quagmire—however fair and inviting and flower-
bedecked the treacherous surface may appear—irom
which we may never be able to extricate ourselves.

In my two previous papers I have given little more -

than a recital of personal experiences and an expres-

sion of opinion based thereon. . Now I purpose dealing

with the views of some of our friends as they appear

No doubt since the first year various othei houses

of various trades, in addition to quartettes and trios,
have been added to the syllabus of the eip  Coeil

TRADITION' AND ITS CHANNELS.

JYRADITION is: the transmission by word of mouth
of things that are recorded in men’s memories.

In the absence of other testimony its authority is para-
mount, but in the presence of authentic written records
its authority is debatable. For it must be remembered
that man’s memory is uncertain, and liable to fault and
failure. The present holder of a tradition may have
a sound memory, and a love for the matter recorded
there, and a desire to preserve it accurately; but he
cannot ensure an equally good memory, and an equal
love for the tradition, in the whole chain of those who
como after. True, a tradition is never, or seldom,
dependent upon one line of persons for its transmission ;
but this circumstance, instead of ensuring perfection in
the tradition, has rather the contrary effect.  The
differences between the recollections of different men in
the same generation issue at last in _wide-branching
diversities. Tt is thus that we get different popular
traditions of historical persous, and of historical events;
it is thus, too, that we get “ variants ”’ of folk-t?les,
and’ much-differing versions of folk-tunes. The diver-
sity of versions: of folk-tales is so great, and the
< yariants?’ so many, that it has led to the setting up
of a science of Comparative Folk-lore. This mere fact

Now, I think that these characteristics are hardly
consistent with much fitness for the faithful and artistic
transmission of musical traditions; they are hardly
consistent with he existence of a very lively w®sthetic
sense. I think that any tunes now in the keeping of
such performers have probably been already corrupted
by them or their forbears, and are likely to be further

I know, of course, that all native Irish singers are
not like those described. I have heard a traditional
singer myself who sang pretty fairly, in a somewhat
chanting sort of manner. The only noticeable
peculiarity about his performance was that he spoke
the last few words of his song. I could see no earthly
reason why the music he sang could not be easily enough
represented by musical notation—by any musician who
really wished to do the thing, not, of course, by one
who wished to show that it could not be done. I once
heard a piper play, too, who was, I believe, a tradi-
tional perforimer. ~ He jigged out a tune in a jaunty,
light manner, which I recognised as the melody of the
“ Wearing of the Green.”’” He played it in such wise
that he managed to efface every vestige of the passion,
poetry, and dignity of the tune. The glorious melody
that had roused millions of Irishmen, when rightly
rendered, was so given that it failed to thrill anybody.
Yet, I suppose, there are those who will contend that
the piper gave the melody rightly, and that Mr.
Ludwig, who roused Irish audiences passionately with
the same melody, always gave it wrongly! Let it be
so. I had rather have the ‘‘ wrong’’ version which
thrills me as a music-lover and rouses me as an Irish-
man, than the ‘“right’’ version jigged out in such a
way as makes me indifferent to the music, and con-
temptuous of the musician. But, perhaps, it was the
words which Mr. Ludwig sang that roused me? No:
it was the music, rendered as he rendered it. Let the
still unrecorded tunes be taken down, I say, as care-
fully and faithfully as possible; musicians like Mr.
Ludwig, Mr. Robert O’Dwyer, and Mr. Arthur Darley,
may be trusted to find the right interpretation of them.
